text.title: Cron Schedule Reference
text.summary: Cron syntax, common schedule expressions, crontab management commands, and when to use systemd timers instead of cron.

section.title: Cron Expression Syntax
section.p: Format: minute (0-59) | hour (0-23) | day-of-month (1-31) | month (1-12) | day-of-week (0-7, both 0 and 7 = Sunday)

rule.statement: Stagger jobs by 5-10 minutes from each other. Never schedule multiple heavy jobs at exactly :00 — this creates simultaneous resource spikes.
rule.rationale: Most admins default to :00 for hourly and :00/:30 for half-hourly. Staggering distributes load and avoids disk/CPU contention between jobs.

list.item: crontab -e — edit crontab for current user

rule.statement: Store the crontab as a file in the project repo (crontab.txt) and manage it with `crontab crontab.txt`. This provides version control and reproducible server setup.
rule.rationale: Crontab edited in-place has no history. A stored file can be restored after accidental deletion, diffed across servers, and committed with the infrastructure code.

rule.statement: Use systemd timers instead of cron when the job needs: journald logging (Persistent=true for missed runs), resource limits (MemoryMax=, CPUQuota=), or dependency ordering (After=, Wants=).
rule.rationale: Cron has no built-in missed-run recovery, no resource limits, and no dependency system. systemd timers provide all three at the cost of two unit files instead of one crontab line.

example.code:
# When to use cron (simple, familiar, portable): 0 3 * * * /home/nero/workspace/scripts/backup.sh >> /var/log/backup.log 2>&1
# When to use systemd timer (needs missed-run recovery): # backup.timer + backup.service with Persistent=true
